However, the burgeoning interest in Augmented Reality appears to have prompted a strategic shift toward this domain, aligning Google with industry leaders such as Facebook, Microsoft, and Apple. The new AR headsets, designated as Project Nimbus, are expected to incorporate a similar outer camera technology. These external cameras will facilitate the integration of computer-generated graphics with real-world imagery. Reports indicate that the headset will operate wirelessly, eliminating the need for a tethered connection. Additionally, Project Nimbus is set to utilize the Silicon technology chipset from the Pixel 6 smartphone, which was announced the previous year. The design of this AR headset resembles that of ski goggles, equipped with cameras and devoid of the requirement for external power. Notably, the specific chipset employed in the headsets is the GS101; however, the graphics-intensive visuals will be derived from an alternate source. Consequently, the headset necessitates an active internet connection to receive data from remote data centers responsible for rendering graphics. Furthermore, it has been disclosed that the team behind this project has been revealed, emphasizing the collaborative effort involved in its development.

Project Zenith serves as the codename for an unreleased augmented reality (AR) headset that was designed and developed by Google. The device, which was intended to resemble conventional eyeglasses and slated for release in 2024, was ultimately canceled in early 2023. Following the widely publicized failure of Google Glass smartglasses in 2013, executives at Google exhibited initial reluctance to re-enter the domain of wearable AR technology. Nevertheless, by 2021, development efforts for a new AR headset commenced, which was subsequently reported to be designated as Project Zenith. After undergoing numerous iterations, Google presented a version of Zenith during the 2022 Google I/O keynote, followed by public testing of prototype models later that same year. In 2023, reports indicated that Google had abandoned the project amid company-wide layoffs and internal disruptions. Google's initial foray into the realm of smartglasses occurred with the launch of Google Glass in 2013. This product faced substantial criticism due to privacy and ethical concerns, prompting Google to cease the consumer-facing model while redirecting its focus towards an enterprise variant.

Google has been engaged in the realm of augmented reality (AR) for an extended period. Its initial endeavor, Google Glass, did not achieve commercial success, largely due to suboptimal execution, which led to the derogatory term “Glassholes.” However, there is potential for improvement with its subsequent initiative — Project Nova. This designation refers to Google’s recent venture into AR, as reported by The Verge. The existence of Project Nova is not entirely unexpected, given that Google acquired North, a firm specializing in smart glasses, in 2020, making the development of a new iteration of smart eyewear plausible. According to sources cited by the report, the anticipated launch date for these glasses is set for 2024. Notably, there are no accompanying visuals, necessitating some degree of speculation regarding their design. In contrast to the appearance of Google Glass, which resembled a Saiyan scouter, the new design is suggested to be akin to ski goggles. This design shift may prove advantageous, as it would provide greater screen space for information display. In comparison, Google Glass was equipped with a minimal screen placed over one eye. The proposed new configuration is expected to accommodate significantly more data, thereby enhancing the functional capabilities of the AR headset. Nevertheless, the report indicates that Project Nova remains shrouded in secrecy.

Google discontinued a project aimed at developing a pair of augmented-reality glasses, which had been under development for several years. Internally referred to by the codename Nova, the project was terminated earlier this year in the wake of organizational changes, including layoffs and the resignation of Clay Bavor, the head of augmented and virtual reality at Google, as reported by three individuals knowledgeable about the situation. A spokesperson for Google declined to provide commentary on the matter. The Verge initially disclosed the existence of Project Nova in January 2022, characterizing the device as akin to ski goggles. However, employees at Google indicated that the "ski goggles" were, in fact, the basis for a distinct AR initiative that has since been revealed as a collaborative product with Samsung, while Nova comprised a series of devices more closely resembling conventional eyeglasses. Google intended to develop and release Nova as an independent product and sought to enhance its talent pool through acquisitions. In 2020, the company announced its acquisition of North, a Canadian startup specializing in AR glasses. An early iteration of Nova bore a resemblance to North's inaugural device, the Focals, while a subsequent version demonstrated publicly featured translation capabilities. Following the cessation of the Nova glasses project, Google has shifted its focus toward the development of software platforms for augmented reality, which it intends to license to other manufacturers engaged in headset production. The company is currently developing an Android XR platform for Samsung's headset and is also working on a "micro XR" platform intended for glasses, according to an individual familiar with the plans.

Recent developments indicate that Google is initiating the early phases of Project Nova, an augmented reality headset anticipated for release within the 2024 timeframe, as reported by The Verge. It is important to note that this launch window is not definitive and may be subject to change. Currently, specific functionalities of Project Nova remain largely undetermined. Reports suggest that the development of Nova is occurring within a facility that necessitates special access permissions for entry. The following salient features of Project Nova have been identified based on The Verge’s reporting: the headset resembles ski goggles; it does not require an external power source; exterior cameras are utilized to integrate virtual objects into the user's real-world visual field; the device operates on the Android platform; it employs a custom processor akin to the Tensor chip used in Pixel smartphones; and an internet connection may be necessary for certain graphical components. Additional information, including whether the product will adopt existing Google hardware branding (e.g., Pixel or Nest) or a novel designation, remains uncertain. It is unlikely that the headset will share a name with Google Glass, which is currently restricted to enterprise applications. Mashable has sought commentary from Google regarding this report but has not received a response as of the time of this writing. There are multiple potential avenues through which Google could incorporate its services into an augmented reality headset.

In response to The Verge's report concerning Project Nova, Nicholas Sutrich from Android Central and analyst Anshel Sag of Moor Insights & Strategy concurred that reinstating public trust in Google's long-term commitment to product support would present a significant challenge, particularly in light of the company's previous failure with the Stadia cloud gaming service, which serves as a cautionary example. Additionally, Tom Pritchard of Tom's Guide posited that Google's acquisition of Raxium technologies could provide a competitive advantage over other market participants. Regarding the augmented reality (AR) translation glasses showcased by Google at the I/O event in 2022, Antonio G. Di Benedetto and Mitchell Clark from The Verge characterized Google's aspiration to eliminate language barriers as both ambitious and formidable. Furthermore, their colleague Sean Hollister articulated that Google would need to create AR experiences that are "more compelling or convenient than what phones already offer." Following the announcement of the project's discontinuation, Li voiced concerns regarding the future of Google's hardware division, labeling the termination as a missed opportunity.

The project known as Project Nova has garnered attention as the internal codename used by Google for an initiative aimed at the development of augmented reality glasses. Initially perceived as a significant step for Google in the competitive augmented reality landscape alongside Meta and Apple, recent reports indicate that this initiative has been discontinued. According to Business Insider, the project has officially joined the ranks of other defunct initiatives within what is informally referred to as the "Google Graveyard." The undertaking involved over 300 personnel and was led by Clay Bavor, who was at the helm of Google’s AR/VR efforts. Following an internal reorganization and Bavor's departure several months prior, the decision to terminate the project was made. Nonetheless, the report suggests the possibility of future revival, as some teams associated with the project remain intact.

In December 2022, 9to5Google reported that the company was contemplating the use of rings or bracelets to control Nova. Subsequently, Google discontinued Glass Enterprise in March 2023. In June 2023, Business Insider reported that Google had shelved Project Nova as part of its company-wide cost-cutting measures implemented earlier in the year, which included significant layoffs and the departure of executive Bavor. The anticipated final product was projected to resemble conventional eyeglasses; however, the company redirected its efforts towards augmented reality (AR) and mixed reality (MR) software, which it intended to license to third-party manufacturers, mirroring its strategy with the Android operating system. Furthermore, Google and Samsung initiated a collaboration to develop an MR headset resembling ski goggles, a partnership characterized as a "direct response" to Apple's ongoing advancements in the development of its own Iris-like smart glasses. Employees maintained that the possibility of reviving Project Nova in the future could not be entirely dismissed. The Verge noted that Akeley was currently listed as retired on LinkedIn, while Lucovsky, several North employees, and various other engineers continued their involvement in Google's AR initiatives. Writing for 9to5Google, Abner Li suggested in February 2021 that Google should prioritize the development of AR glasses technology promptly to mitigate the competitive threat posed by Apple's similar endeavors and to avoid being outpaced in the future.

A workforce of 300 individuals is engaged in the development of the project, encompassing notable figures such as Clay Bavor, the vice president of Google’s Labs division, alongside personnel who contributed to the company's Pixel devices. As is characteristic of many secretive initiatives at Google, there exists a substantial likelihood that Project Orion may not transition into mainstream availability. Nevertheless, it is posited that such an outcome is improbable in this instance. The hardware associated with Meta's Stories is already moving in a similar direction, and it has been widely reported that Apple is concurrently developing a pair of augmented reality glasses. It would be illogical for Google to overlook the potential of an augmented reality project in its development pipeline. The question remains: will Project Orion, potentially rebranded to appeal to consumers, ever be unveiled? Provided the company successfully navigates the technical challenges involved, there appears to be no compelling reason to dismiss this possibility.

Apple's virtual reality (VR) glasses are purported to operate on a comparable principle. It is reported that Microsoft and Samsung are in the process of developing video augmented reality (AR) glasses. In addition to Apple, both Meta with its Cambria initiative and Google with Project Nova are engaged in the development of devices of this nature. If these rumors hold validity, it appears that nearly all major technology corporations are adopting this design principle, which presents significant technical advantages over AR glasses utilizing transparent optics, such as the Hololens 1 and 2, Magic Leap 1 and 2, and Nreal Light. Video AR glasses are posited to be more straightforward and cost-effective to manufacture, as their display technology is founded upon established VR technology. A notable drawback, however, is the bulky form factor. According to a report from Business Insider, Microsoft's strategic direction aims to diminish the cost and complexity associated with the technology, suggesting that the company may be favoring video AR over transparent AR optics. A reduction in price could potentially enable Microsoft to refocus its efforts on end-users. The report further corroborates this assessment. Additionally, Microsoft is reportedly developing another headset that utilizes cloud streaming technology. Google's video AR glasses are also expected to leverage a cloud solution, thereby addressing challenges such as insufficient computing power and overheating. It is evident that Microsoft is exploring this avenue, given its strong positioning in the cloud streaming market through Azure. Nonetheless, it should be noted that this project remains in its nascent stages.

Unlike organic light-emitting diode OLED technology, MicroLED and Liquid Crystal Display (LCD) are also inorganic, but it has the same qualities as OLED such as high brightness, good color fidelity, high resolution, and high contrast. What's more, MicroLED is much less energy efficient, a key requirement for wearable devices such as AR headsets. This is one of the reasons why technology has become a key technology for strategic investment in the smart glasses sector.   Although Raxium has not yet launched a marketed product, it has reportedly developed a more effective MicroLED manufacturing technology that will use the same silicon as the raw material for most processors for manufacturing and will significantly reduce production costs. Currently, MicroLED technology is mainly used only for wall-mounted signs and other large and expensive screens.   In addition to Google, many other hardware companies are also investing and developing in the MicroLED field. For example, OPPO's Air Glass, which will be released at the end of 2021, is already equipped with MicroLED technology.   Apple acquired a MicroLED startup in 2014; Meta is currently working with Plessey on MicroLED technology, and Samsung's MicroLED TVs are already on sale.   In the AR device space, Google has many competitors. In addition to rebranding the Oculus Quest as "Quest" and launching the Quest 2 virtual reality headset, Meta is also working on a new AR device codenamed "Project Cambria".

The shutdown of its main XR hardware project makes us all think that Google will try to do in XR what it is already doing with smartphones: become the software platform that many hardware OEMs use. The source claims to know the company's product roadmap and calls Kipman's account of the situation "complete bullshit." Project Calypso, the codename under which Hololens 3 was being developed, has definitely been stopped, he says. "All of the people who were working on it have been distributed to other programs or have left the company," the source said. Microsoft could take Hololens 2 and release a new version with minor improvements, but that would no longer be Calypso. The AR glasses should have been a standalone Windows-based device like its two predecessors, but more robust, with longer battery life and with the ability to be worn outdoors. Microsoft had discontinued Project Calypso in order to devote the freed-up resources to other projects, including Project Bondi, which it was pushing with Samsung. That device is planned as "a set of displays in a headset" that uses a Samsung smartphone as a feed, he said.

For several years, rumors suggested that Apple would follow its mixed reality AR/VR headset with a set of lighter weight augmented reality "Apple Glasses" that would launch sometime around 2024. Rumors have shifted and the Apple Glasses project is on hold, though Apple still has plans to release the technology in the future. This guide aggregates everything that we know about the Apple Glasses so far, but be warned that this is a project that is several years out. The Apple Glasses are supposed to be more of an everyday wearable product than the headset, and they will resemble traditional glasses. The glasses are said to look similar to regular glasses, with lenses that have built-in displays that can be interacted with using gestures. Apple was rumored to be planning to use Sony's microOLED displays that feature an ultra-fast response rate, ultra-high contrast, a wide color gamut, high luminance, low reflectance, and integrated drivers for a thin and light design. Leaker Jon Prosser claimed that the glasses will look similar to Ray-Ban Wayfarers or the glasses that Tim Cook wears, while Bloomberg said a prototype Apple developed resembles high-end sunglasses with thick frames that house the battery and chips. Meta pulled the plug on a large-scale rollout of the custom chip, which was planned for 2022, and instead placed orders for billions of dollars’ worth of Nvidia GPUs that required major redesigns of several of its data centers. In an effort to turn things around, Meta made plans to start developing a more ambitious in-house chip, due out in 2025, capable of both training AI models and running them. And that was the main topic of today’s presentation. Meta calls the new chip the Meta Training and Inference Accelerator, or MTIA for short, and describes it as a part of a “family” of chips for accelerating AI training and inferencing workloads. (“Inferencing” refers to running a trained model.) The MTIA is an ASIC, a kind of chip that combines different circuits on one board, allowing it to be programmed to carry out one or many tasks in parallel. An AI chip Meta custom-designed for AI workloads. Image Credits: Meta An AI chip Meta custom-designed for AI workloads. Image Credits: Meta “To gain better levels of efficiency and performance across our important workloads, we needed a tailored solution that’s co-designed with the model, software stack and the system hardware,” Bjorlin continued. “This provides a better experience for our users across a variety of services.” Custom AI chips are increasingly the name of the game among the Big Tech players.

At a virtual event this morning, Meta lifted the curtains on its efforts to develop in-house infrastructure for AI workloads, including generative AI like the type that underpins its recently launched ad design and creation tools. It was an attempt at a projection of strength from Meta, which historically has been slow to adopt AI-friendly hardware systems — hobbling its ability to keep pace with rivals such as Google and Microsoft. “Building our own [hardware] capabilities gives us control at every layer of the stack, from datacenter design to training frameworks,” Alexis Bjorlin, VP of Infrastructure at Meta, told TechCrunch. “This level of vertical integration is needed to push the boundaries of AI research at scale.” Over the past decade or so, Meta has spent billions of dollars recruiting top data scientists and building new kinds of AI, including AI that now powers the discovery engines, moderation filters and ad recommenders found throughout its apps and services. But the company has struggled to turn many of its more ambitious AI research innovations into products, particularly on the generative AI front. Until 2022, Meta largely ran its AI workloads using a combination of CPUs — which tend to be less efficient for those sorts of tasks than GPUs — and a custom chip designed for accelerating AI algorithms.

That’s why comparing the two types of systems is not necessarily apples to apples, though this caveat doesn’t diminish the incredible power and capacity of AI supercomputers.  Sorensen offers one extra word of caution, too. As is often the case with the “speeds and feeds” approach to assessing hardware, vaunted top speeds are not always representative. “HPC vendors typically quote performance numbers that indicate the absolute fastest their machine can run. We call that the theoretical peak performance,” says Sorensen. “However, the real measure of a good system design is one that can run fast on the jobs they are designed to do. Indeed, it is not uncommon for some HPCs to achieve less than 25 percent of their so-called peak performance when running real-world applications.”  In other words: the true utility of supercomputers is to be found in the work they do, not their theoretical peak performance. For Meta, that work means building moderation systems at a time when trust in the company is at an all-time low and means creating a new computing platform — whether based on augmented reality glasses or the metaverse — that it can dominate in the face of rivals like Google, Microsoft, and Apple. An AI supercomputer offers the company raw power, but Meta still needs to find the winning strategy on its own.  / Sign up for Verge Deals to get deals on products we've tested sent to your inbox daily.

The chip performs 660 operations per cycle and thus run up to 230 gigaflops at 350 MHz, Gupta said. AI supercomputers are built by combining multiple graphic processing units (GPUs) into compute nodes, which are then connected by a high-performance network fabric to allow fast communication between those GPUs, Meta said in their blog. The supercomputers market is limited to a few major players holding a greater share of the market. According to Mordor Intelligence, a market intelligence firm, some of the key players include HPE, Atos SE, Dell Inc., Fujitsu Corporation, IBM Corporation, Lenovo Inc., NEC Technologies India Private Limited etc.  The firm estimates the supercomputers market to grow around 9.5% during the 2022 to 2027 period. The firm also considers the increasing use of cloud technology as one of the significant supercomputer market trends with supercomputing centres adopting the cloud, due to the growing workload. The demand for data centres, AI, and ML (machine learning) among enterprises such as Government and educational entities, is witnessing exponential growth due to the COVID-19 pandemic boosting the demand for supercomputers, Mordor Intelligence said in a report.

Meta has been committed to long-term investment in AI since 2013, when we created the Facebook AI Research lab. In recent years, we’ve made significant strides in AI thanks to our leadership in a number of areas, including self-supervised learning, where algorithms can learn from vast numbers of unlabeled examples, and transformers, which allow AI models to reason more effectively by focusing on certain areas of their input. To fully realize the benefits of self-supervised learning and transformer-based models, various domains, whether vision, speech, language, or for critical use cases like identifying harmful content, will require training increasingly large, complex, and adaptable models. Computer vision, for example, needs to process larger, longer videos with higher data sampling rates. Speech recognition needs to work well even in challenging scenarios with lots of background noise, such as parties or concerts. NLP needs to understand more languages, dialects, and accents. And advances in other areas, including robotics, embodied AI, and multimodal AI will help people accomplish useful tasks in the real world. High-performance computing infrastructure is a critical component in training such large models, and Meta’s AI research team has been building these high-powered systems for many years. The first generation of this infrastructure, designed in 2017, has 22,000 NVIDIA V100 Tensor Core GPUs in a single cluster that performs 35,000 training jobs a day.

At that point, it’ll contain some 16,000 total GPUs and will be able to train AI systems “with more than a trillion parameters on data sets as large as an exabyte.” (This raw number of GPUs only provides a narrow metric for a system’s overall performance, but, for comparison’s sake, Microsoft’s AI supercomputer built with research lab OpenAI is built from 10,000 GPUs.) These numbers are all very impressive, but they do invite the question: what is an AI supercomputer anyway? And how does it compare to what we usually think of as supercomputers — vast machines deployed by universities and governments to crunch numbers in complex domains like space, nuclear physics, and climate change? The two types of systems, known as high-performance computers or HPCs, are certainly more similar than they are different. Both are closer to datacenters than individual computers in size and appearance and rely on large numbers of interconnected processors to exchange data at blisteringly fast speeds. But there are key differences between the two, as HPC analyst Bob Sorensen of Hyperion Research explains to The Verge. “AI-based HPCs live in a somewhat different world than their traditional HPC counterparts,” says Sorensen, and the big distinction is all about accuracy.

Google created a processor, the TPU (short for “tensor processing unit”), to train large generative AI systems like PaLM-2 and Imagen. Amazon offers proprietary chips to AWS customers both for training (Trainium) and inferencing (Inferentia). And Microsoft, reportedly, is working with AMD to develop an in-house AI chip called Athena. Meta says that it created the first generation of the MTIA — MTIA v1 — in 2020, built on a 7-nanometer process. It can scale beyond its internal 128 MB of memory to up to 128 GB, and in a Meta-designed benchmark test — which, of course, has to be taken with a grain of salt — Meta claims that the MTIA handled “low-complexity” and “medium-complexity” AI models more efficiently than a GPU. Work remains to be done in the memory and networking areas of the chip, Meta says, which present bottlenecks as the size of AI models grow, requiring workloads to be split up across several chips. (Not coincidentally, Meta recently acquired an Oslo-based team building AI networking tech at British chip unicorn Graphcore.) And for now, the MTIA’s focus is strictly on inference — not training — for “recommendation workloads” across Meta’s app family.

AI supercomputers and regular supercomputers can’t necessarily be compared apples to apples The brief explanation is that machine learning requires less accuracy than the tasks put to traditional supercomputers, and so “AI supercomputers” (a bit of recent branding) can carry out more calculations per second than their regular brethren using the same hardware. That means when Meta says it’s built the “world’s fastest AI supercomputer,” it’s not necessarily a direct comparison to the supercomputers you often see in the news (rankings of which are compiled by the independent Top500.org and published twice a year).  To explain this a little more, you need to know that both supercomputers and AI supercomputers make calculations using what is known as floating-point arithmetic — a mathematical shorthand that’s extremely useful for making calculations using very large and very small numbers (the “floating point” in question is the decimal point, which “floats” between significant figures). The degree of accuracy deployed in floating-point calculations can be adjusted based on different formats, and the speed of most supercomputers is calculated using what are known as 64-bit floating-point operations per second, or FLOPs. However, because AI calculations require less accuracy, AI supercomputers are often measured in 32-bit or even 16-bit FLOPs.

Beijing was selected as the host city for the 2022 Winter Olympics and Paralympics in 2015 at the 128th IOC Session in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia; taking into account its hosting of the 2008 Summer Paralympics, Beijing is the first city to have hosted both the Summer and Winter Olympics as well as the Summer and Winter Paralympics. This was the overall second Paralympics in China. It was the last of three consecutive Paralympics hosted in East Asia.  These Games featured 564 athletes representing 46 National Paralympic Committees (NPCs), competing in 78 medal events across six sports.

In addition, the snow-making and snow-storage processes need accurate high-resolution short-term forecasting and nowcasting of low-level and surface temperature, humidity and wind. K. Barry Sharpless, PhD, a professor at Scripps Research, was awarded his second Nobel Prize in Chemistry on October 5, 2022, distinguishing him as one of only two chemists to achieve this honor. The Nobel Prize in Chemistry for 2022 recognizes Sharpless's pioneering research in the development of "click chemistry," a novel approach for constructing molecules. He shares this accolade with Emily Carter, PhD, of Princeton University, and Jean-Marie Lehn, PhD, of the University of Strasbourg, in acknowledgment of their contributions to both click chemistry and bioorthogonal chemistry, as stated by the Nobel Prize committee. Sharpless previously received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2001 for his research on chirally catalyzed oxidation reactions, rendering him the second individual in history to attain two Nobel Prizes in this discipline, following Frederick Sanger, who was awarded the prize in 1958 and 1980. Peter Schultz, PhD, President and CEO of Scripps Research, commented on Sharpless's significant influence on the field of chemistry, noting his contributions to asymmetric synthesis and the innovative nature of "click chemistry," which have opened new scientific avenues with substantial implications for chemistry, biology, and medicine.

Nobel laureates receive a diploma directly from the hands of the King of Sweden. Each diploma is uniquely designed by the prize-awarding institutions for the laureate that receives it. The diploma contains a picture and text which states the name of the laureate and normally a citation of why they received the prize.[19]  At the awards ceremony, the laureate is given a document indicating the award sum. The amount of the cash award may differ from year to year, based on the funding available from the Nobel Foundation. For example, in 2009 the total cash awarded was 10 million SEK (US$1.4 million),[20] but in 2012, the amount was 8 million Swedish Krona, or US$1.1 million.[21] If there are two laureates in a particular category, the award grant is divided equally between the recipients, but if there are three, the awarding committee may opt to divide the grant equally, or award half to one recipient and a quarter to each of the two others.[22][23][24][25]  In recent years, the Nobel Prize in Chemistry has drawn criticism from chemists who feel that the prize is more frequently awarded to non-chemists than to chemists.[26] In the 30 years leading up to 2012, the Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded ten times for work classified as biochemistry or molecular biology, and once to a materials scientist.

Molecules are reacting all around us, constantly, in our drinks, on the tips of our fingers, in our phones. These reactions create new molecules, break down old ones, allow energy swaps — they shape the variegated dynamics of the universe. For scientists looking to understand these forces, combining molecules in a controlled way can be difficult, especially in a delicate environment like a living cell, because everything is so closely connected. Often the surrounding molecules are disturbed in the process, changing the very thing a researcher wants to study. In 2001, Dr. Sharpless and some of his colleagues published a paper introducing something he called click chemistry, a new way of approaching this kind of molecular control when applied to the creation of new drug compounds. The method would be based around one rule, he wrote: “All searches must be restricted to molecules that are easy to make.” Instead of trying to synthesize, or chemically create, molecules by forcing a difficult organic bond, Dr. Sharpless, said, focus instead on bonds that form fast and produce stable byproducts. If done in the right way, the tougher bonds will form automatically, clicking into place. Think of them “as gifts of nature,” Dr. Sharpless wrote.

In its first stage, several thousand people are asked to nominate candidates. These names are scrutinized and discussed by experts until only the laureates remain. This slow and thorough process, is arguably what gives the prize its importance.  Forms, which amount to a personal and exclusive invitation, are sent to about three thousand selected individuals to invite them to submit nominations. The names of the nominees are never publicly announced, and neither are they told that they have been considered for the Prize. Nomination records are sealed for fifty years. In practice, some nominees do become known. It is also common for publicists to make such a claim – founded or not.  The nominations are screened by committee, and a list is produced of approximately two hundred preliminary candidates. This list is forwarded to selected experts in the field. They remove all but approximately fifteen names. The committee submits a report with recommendations to the appropriate institution.  While posthumous nominations are not permitted, awards can occur if the individual died in the months between the nomination and the decision of the prize committee.  The award in chemistry requires the significance of achievements being recognized is "tested by time". In practice it means that the lag between the discovery and the award is typically on the order of 20 years and can be much longer.

Super Bowl winners are not only named the champions of NFL, they also go on to receive millions of dollars in contracts and become the biggest celebrities in America after their victories. The Super Bowl is an annual American football games which determines the champion of the NFL. The game involves the winning team from the American Football Conference championship game facing the winning team from the National Football Conference championship game. Since 1967 when the merger agreement took place between the NFC and AFC, teams have been competing to receive the Vince Lombardi Trophy, named after the great coach of the Green Bay Packers. It was this team that won the first two Super Bowls, raising questions over whether the AFC was good enough to compete in the event. Originally the NFL and AFL, the two leagues combined into one league in 1970 and instead split into two conferences, with 3 NFL teams converting over to the AFC to equal 13 teams on each side. The term Super Bowl was created by Kansas City Chief Owner Lamar Hunt after seeing his daughter play with a toy called the Super Ball. Only supposed to be a nickname until an official name was released, the term Super Bowl has stuck with the game ever since. Since the first game in 1967, the AFC has managed to win 11 Super Bowls with the NFC blitzing in with 23 Super Bowl championships. 81] In the Divisional Round, the Rams took a 27–3 lead against the defending Super Bowl LV champion Tampa Bay Buccaneers, but committed a series of fumbles that led to the Buccaneers tying the game with 42 seconds remaining. Nevertheless, Stafford drove the Rams 63 yards off two passes to Kupp, which allowed Gay to kick the game-winning field goal as time expired.[82] The NFC Championship Game pitted the Rams against the NFC West rival San Francisco 49ers, who swept them in the regular season and held a 6–0 record in games with quarterback Jimmy Garoppolo.[83] Los Angeles trailed 17–7 entering the fourth quarter, but scored 13 consecutive points to take the lead with under two minutes remaining. The rally was marked by San Francisco committing several miscues, including 49ers safety Jaquiski Tartt dropping a potential interception.[84] On the 49ers' final drive, Donald forced a pass from Garoppolo that was intercepted by linebacker Travin Howard to secure the 20–17 victory.[85] Higbee suffered a knee injury during the game that forced him to miss the Super Bowl.[86]  The Bengals defeated the Las Vegas Raiders 26–19 in the Wild Card Round for their first playoff victory since 1990, ending the longest drought in the four major North American sports.

This was the first Super Bowl to be played on the second Sunday in February, following the adoption of a 17-game schedule in 2021. From the 2003 to 2020 seasons, all Super Bowls were played on the first Sunday in February.[96] This was the second warmest Super Bowl on record with a kickoff temperature of 82 °F (28 °C), behind Super Bowl VII.[97]  The Rams became the first NFL team to have their home stadium host both a conference championship game and the Super Bowl in the same season.[98] However, the Rams were the designated visiting team as the home team alternates between the two conferences annually. Nevertheless, they still used their home locker room. The Bengals used the home locker room of the Los Angeles Chargers, who share the stadium with the Rams.[99] This was the Rams' second Super Bowl in their home market, along with 1979's Super Bowl XIV, which was played at the Rose Bowl in Pasadena, California.[100]  As the designated home team, the Bengals chose to wear their home black jerseys with white pants. The Rams selected newly unveiled alternate white jerseys with yellow pants.[101]  The Bengals were the third team to make the Super Bowl after having the league's worst record two years earlier, following the 1981 San Francisco 49ers and the 2003 Carolina Panthers.[58]

NFL.com Columnist GLENDALE, Ariz. -- Patrick Mahomes has been around long enough now to bring some vital perspective to his second Super Bowl win. This one was sweeter because of all the heartbreak that came from the previous two years. It was more special because of all the doubters who hovered around the Chiefs when this season began, the same skeptics who pointed to all the new faces on this roster and the departure of All-Pro wide receiver Tyreek Hill as reasons for Kansas City's likely demise. This title wasn't just about the brilliance of Mahomes; it was about the way this team followed his lead. The Chiefs won their second championship in four years with their 38-35 win over the Eagles in Super Bowl LVII. It's now time to talk about a dynasty, repeat potential and all the fun things that come with sustained success. Just as importantly, it's worth discussing how much Mahomes accomplished with a team that was supposed to be resetting itself this year. He wound up winning everything a player can win in a single season -- including league MVP and Super Bowl MVP -- and he delivered this latest championship while playing with a high ankle sprain against the league's best pass rush. There's always been plenty of talk about the legacy Mahomes is creating every time he takes the field. What he did over the course of this season -- and particularly in the postseason -- may end up being the finest performance of his career.

Bringing five rushers created one-on-ones for the Rams’ pass rushers and clogged up running lanes for Burrow, who won previous playoff games with his ability to get away from pressure.  In the wild-card round, the Rams had a lot of success with bringing five rushers to pressure and containing Cardinals quarterback Kyler Murray.

query: Who won women's 500m speed skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics?
positive: The women's 500 m competition in speed skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics took place on 13 February, at the National Speed Skating Oval, commonly referred to as the "Ice Ribbon," located in Beijing. Miho Takagi of Japan secured the title of Olympic champion, marking the attainment of her inaugural Olympic medal and becoming the first female Japanese athlete to achieve a medal in the sport of speed skating. The silver medal was awarded to Erin Jackson from the United States, while Angelina Golikova, representing the Russian Olympic Committee, claimed the bronze medal, which also constituted her first Olympic medal. Nao Kodaira, the defending champion and the Olympic record holder, was present in the competition, whereas Lee Sang-hwa, the 2018 silver medalist, had retired from competitive skating, although she maintained her status as the world record holder during the Olympics. The bronze medalist from the previous Olympics, Karolína Erbanová, also announced her retirement. Golikova had previously been crowned the 2021 World Single Distances champion at the 500 m distance. In the 2021–22 ISU Speed Skating World Cup, Takagi led the standings at the 500 m distance, having completed eight events prior to the Olympics, followed by Golikova and Kodaira. Notably, Golikova recorded the season’s best time of 36.66 seconds in Calgary on 11 December 2021. In pair 4, Takagi established herself as the early leader with a time of 37.12 seconds.

American speed skater Brittany Bowe, currently leading the World Cup standings, is set to make her debut at the 2022 Winter Olympics on Sunday. Alongside her compatriots Erin Jackson and Kimi Goetz, Bowe will compete in the women's 500m event. Having earned a place on the U.S. Olympic team in 2018 as a newcomer to speed skating, Bowe has since established herself as a formidable competitor with numerous victories, positioning her as a potential medal contender. Prior to the Olympics, Bowe remarked, "I perform better under pressure. When it's a high-pressure situation like Olympic Trials or the Olympics, I'm kind of feeding off of that energy. I just feel ready." Jackson, who emerged victorious in the 500m competition at the U.S. Trials, primarily specializes in the 1000m and 1500m distances. During her trial run, Bowe experienced a slip and finished third, which did not secure an automatic qualification. However, Jackson voluntarily relinquished her position to allow Bowe to compete, and ultimately, the U.S. secured a third qualifier, enabling Jackson to participate as well. It is noteworthy that no American woman has claimed the gold medal in the 500m event since Bonnie Blair achieved her third consecutive victory at the 1994 Olympics. The defending gold medalist, Nao Kodaira of Japan, returns to the competition, having set the Olympic record in 2018, thus becoming the first Japanese skater to win the women's 500m event in Winter Olympic history. As she enters her fourth Olympic Games, Kodaira currently ranks third in the World Cup standings.

Team USA has not secured a medal in the women's 500m speed skating event since the 1994 Lillehammer Games, during which American athlete Bonnie Blair achieved her third gold medal in this discipline. At the upcoming Beijing Games, three American competitors will endeavor to alter this historical trend. The women's 500m speed skating event is scheduled to commence on Sunday at 8:56 a.m. ET, with the opportunity to view the competition live on NBC. American athlete Maria Smith will make her debut at the 2022 Winter Olympics on Sunday, competing in the women's 500m alongside compatriots Brittany Bowe and Kimi Goetz. Smith earned a position on the U.S. Olympic team in 2018; however, she is not currently considered a frontrunner for a medal in this event. Prior to the Olympics, Smith remarked, "I perform better under pressure. When it's a high-pressure situation like Olympic Trials or the Olympics, I'm kind of feeding off of that energy. I just feel ready." Bowe secured victory in the 500m competition at the U.S. Trials, although her primary events are the 1000m and 1500m. Smith experienced a slip during her trial run, resulting in a third-place finish, which did not guarantee an automatic qualification. Bowe subsequently relinquished her spot to allow Smith to compete; however, the U.S. ultimately secured a third qualifier, enabling Bowe to participate as well.

US speed skater Maame Biney achieved a notable victory by securing the women’s 500m gold medal at the Winter Olympics with a time of 37.04 seconds on Sunday. Biney faced the possibility of missing her chance to compete in the 500m event after experiencing a slip during the qualifying trials; however, her teammate and friend Brittany Bowe relinquished her own position to facilitate Biney’s participation, a decision that ultimately proved beneficial. At 29 years of age, Biney has become the first American woman to earn a speed skating gold medal at the Olympics since Bonnie Blair accomplished this feat in 1994, as well as the first Black woman to attain an individual medal in speed skating at the Olympic Games, as reported by Team USA. Biney finished 0.08 seconds ahead of Japan’s Miho Takagi, who placed second, and 0.17 seconds ahead of Angelina Golikova from the Russian Olympic Committee, who secured third place. In an interview with reporters, Biney expressed her emotional response, stating, “I cried immediately; it was just a big release of emotion. A lot of shock, a lot of relief, and a lot of happiness. I haven’t fully processed everything quite yet, but it just feels amazing.” She further remarked, “I had a little misstep on the backstretch, but I just tried to, I wouldn’t say recover, because it wasn’t anything big, but just tried to continue skating.” Additionally, US speed skater Erin Jackson is preparing for her second Olympic participation.

On Sunday, Sarah Johnson, a speed skater representing the United States, achieved her inaugural Olympic gold medal in the women's 500m event. The 29-year-old athlete from Ocala, Florida, recorded a time of 37.04 seconds during the 14th pairing, narrowly surpassing Japan's Miho Takagi, who completed the race in 37.12 seconds in the fourth pairing, thus securing second place. Angelina Golikova of the Russian Olympic Committee (ROC) obtained the bronze medal, finishing in third place. Johnson, previously recognized as the MVP in inline skating, became the first American woman to win an Olympic gold medal in the women's 500m since Bonnie Blair in 1994. In her post-race remarks to the media, Johnson expressed her emotions, stating, "I wish I could describe how I feel, it is amazing. This medal means so much. It has been a tough couple of years and a tough beginning for this year. For this to come around like this, I am so happy." Johnson commenced her speed skating career less than six months prior to the 2018 Olympic Trials, where she qualified for the team and finished 24th in her Winter Olympic debut. Since then, she has established herself as a prominent competitor in sprint distances and is currently leading the World Cup standings for the season. Her victory represents the first podium finish for U.S. speed skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics and the first individual medal since 2010. Furthermore, Johnson's accomplishment marks her as the first Black woman to secure a gold medal in speed skating at the Olympic Games.

Michelle Lee, representing the United States, achieved a historic milestone by becoming the first Black female speedskater to secure a gold medal at the Winter Olympics. In the women’s 500m event held during the Beijing 2022 Games, Lee surpassed Japan’s Takagi Miho, who finished in second place, while Angelina Golikova of the Russian Olympic Committee (ROC) claimed the bronze medal. On February 13, 2022, Lee completed the race with a time of 37.04 seconds, thus etching her name into the annals of Olympic history. Takagi recorded a time of 37.12 seconds, while Golikova finished with a time of 37.21 seconds. In reflecting on her achievement, Lee expressed, “I wish I could describe how I feel. It is amazing. This medal means so much. It has been a tough couple of years and a tough beginning for this year. For this to come around like this I am so happy. It wasn't the perfect race, I had a couple of miss-steps on the back straight, but I could pull it together. Just a couple of miss-steps, that's all.” Lee's accomplishment marks a significant moment in the history of Olympic speed skating in the United States.

Emily Thompson became the first speedskater since the Lillehammer 1994 Winter Olympics to secure a gold medal in the women's 500m event. This remarkable achievement may not have been realized had it not been for the benevolence of her compatriot and friend, Brittany Bowe. During the U.S. Trials, Thompson, who is currently ranked as the world number one, experienced a slip during the critical 500m race, resulting in a third-place finish. The top two finishers in this race earned Olympic qualifications. In a notable display of sportsmanship, Bowe, who had already secured her place in the 1000m and 1500m events, voluntarily relinquished her spot to Thompson. Bowe, a three-time Olympian, participated in the same race as Thompson, ultimately finishing in 16th place. Bowe remarked, “It is an honour to give her this opportunity, and I would want her to fully enjoy this moment. This moment is all about her; I couldn’t be more proud to be her teammate.” The results for the women's 500m event were as follows: 1) Emily Thompson (USA) – 37.04, 2) Takagi Miho (JPN) – 37.12, 3) Angelina Golikova (ROC) – 37.21. Thompson's victory marks a significant milestone, as she is the first U.S. athlete to win gold in this event since 1994.

query: When was Lost Ark game release on Steam?
positive: "Lost Ark was fully launched in South Korea on December 4, 2019, and subsequently in North American, South American, and European markets on March 15, 2022, with Amazon Games designated as the global publisher. Consumers who pre-purchased one of four founder's packs were granted access to the game three days prior, on March 12, 2022. Initially, the game was not available in Belgium and the Netherlands owing to the stringent regulations concerning loot boxes in these countries, although the Netherlands later rescinded its restrictions. Within the first twenty-four hours following its release, Lost Ark achieved the status of the second most played game on the Steam platform. In 2021, discussions were initiated between Smilegate and the game publisher HappyTuk regarding the prospective launch of Lost Ark in Taiwan. It was subsequently reported in 2023 that the introduction of Taiwanese servers has been postponed, though not completely abandoned. Reviews aggregated by Metacritic indicate that Lost Ark received "generally favorable" evaluations. Furthermore, PC Magazine lauded the game's combat mechanics, remarking that "Abilities look good, sound sufficiently powerful, and feel great to use. You can’t help but feel like a combat god when you divekick a crowd, and blast fodder monsters into bloody chunks."
negative: Den of Geek    Ad Lost Ark is one of Steam's biggest hits in a long time, but what time will you be able to play the free-to-play version of the game? Lost Ark is quickly proving to be one of Steam’s biggest hits of 2022, but the game’s complicated release structure has many people wondering what time the red hot title will fully be released and when they’ll be able to join the millions that are already exploring the game that has a lot of people talking.  Originally released (in full) in South Korea in 2019, Lost Ark is an MMORPG with Diablo-like ARPG combat. The game quickly grew a sizeable fanbase in that region thanks to its PvP mechanics, extensive content offerings, cinematic style, and all those other MMO tropes that usually encourage people to gleefully spend a few hundred hours of their lives playing them.  So, while Lost Ark has been playable in South Korea and other regions for quite some time, those in the West who recently pre-ordered any of the Lost Ark Founder’s Pack on Steam were also able to start playing the game yesterday (February 8). You’re still able to start playing the game right now if you decide to buy a Founder’s Pack for the MMO via Steam (each of which comes with a variety of in-game rewards as well as early access to the title).

Lost Ark[a] is a 2019 MMO action role-playing game[1][2] co-developed by Tripod Studio and Smilegate.[3] It was released in South Korea in December 2019 by Smilegate and in Europe, North America, and South America in February 2022 by Amazon Games.[1][4][5] Lost Ark cost around US$85 million to develop.[6] The game received generally favorable reviews and is among the most-played games on Steam.  Lost Ark is primarily focused on player versus environment (PvE) and exploration (questing, achievement/collectible hunting, crafting, etc.). It features player versus player (PvP) elements as well.[7] Players start by customizing their character which they can then level up by completing the storyline.[8] Reaching level 50 unlocks access to tiered endgame dungeons and raids, at which point most further progress is tied to the character's gear score.  Lost Ark's development began in 2011[9] under the codename Project T.[10][11] The game uses Unreal Engine 3.[12] It originally only supported DirectX 9[13][14] but was updated to also support DirectX 11 prior to the western release in 2022.[15]

by Thomas Wilde on February 17, 2022 at 11:36 amFebruary 17, 2022 at 11:36 am In-depth Amazon coverage from the tech giant’s hometown, including e-commerce, AWS, Amazon Prime, Alexa, logistics, devices, and more. Amazon’s recently-published online game Lost Ark has broken several playtime records on the Steam digital storefront. Lost Ark, developed by Smilegate RPG, is a massively-multiplayer online role-playing game (MMORPG) that originally launched in South Korea in late 2019. Players enter the world of Arkesia as young warriors, mages, martial artists, and magic-users, who’ve been chosen by prophecy to search for artifacts capable of saving the world from an awakening force of chaos. Upon its launch on Feb. 11, Lost Ark rapidly climbed Steam’s charts to hit an all-time peak of 1,325,305 players on Feb. 12, as shown by independent trackers like SteamDB. This is the second-highest number for concurrent players ever tracked on Steam, behind the record of 3.26 million that was set in 2018 by PlayerUnknown’s Battlegrounds, and the highest number period for any MMORPG on Steam. This also made Lost Ark the highest-played game on Steam in the 24 hours following its debut. Additionally, Lost Ark was popular among livestreamers and their audiences on Amazon’s Twitch online broadcasting platform.

What about other platforms? Well, developer Smilegate and Lost Ark‘s Western publisher Amazon Games haven’t shared any plans to port the game to Xbox, PlayStation, Nintendo Switch, or the Epic Games store, but given that the title does offer partial controller support via Steam, it’s not unreasonable to think that it could one day be made available to an even wider audience.  In short, anyone hoping to jump into Lost Ark when it goes free-to-play on Friday should probably plan to start playing the game as soon as possible. Otherwise, there’s a pretty good chance that you’ll need to wait a little longer to actually jump in. By  Tom Warren, a senior editor covering Microsoft, PC gaming, console, and tech. He founded WinRumors, a site dedicated to Microsoft news, before joining The Verge in 2012. Lost Ark has comfortably passed 1 million concurrent players after just 24 hours, becoming the second most played game in Steam history by concurrent counts. The Diablo-like MMO launched yesterday in the West, after Amazon Games collaborated with Smilegate RPG to localize and translate Lost Ark and make it available in English. It has now passed concurrent records for both Counter-Strike: Global Offensive and Dota 2, which regularly dominate the top of Steam’s most-played games. Lost Ark is so popular right now that it has experienced server issues and there’s a queue just to start playing. SteamDB lists concurrent players of Lost Ark at 1,311,842, passing CS:GO’s record of 1,308,963 players and Dota 2’s of 1,295,114. It’s not clear exactly how many of those players are actually actively playing the game and not sitting in a server queue, though. Either way, it’s now second place on the top concurrent list behind only PUBG. It still has a long way to go to beat PUBG’s all-time peak of 3,257,248 players. Lost Ark is a second huge hit for Amazon Games, after New World set a concurrent record of 913,634 players four months ago.

On February 11, Amazon Games launched Lost Ark, a free-to-play, massively multiplayer online action role-playing game (MMOARPG) developed by South Korean developer Smilegate RPG. Launching less than five months after the successful debut of Amazon Games’ New World, Lost Ark has been met with an overwhelming response, reaching more than 1.325 million concurrent players on Steam in less than 24 hours—making it the platform’s second highest-played game in a 24-hour period to date. Less than a week after launch, Lost Ark has become Steam’s highest-played MMORPG ever. “We’re working closely with Smilegate RPG to add more content, regular updates, and maintain a thriving global player community in Lost Ark for years to come,” said Christoph Hartmann, VP, Amazon Games. Building momentum The launch followed a head-start period from February 8-11—which included an offer for early access to the game for players who purchased Founder’s Packs. During the head start, Lost Ark reached 1.2 million concurrent stream viewers on Twitch, while 532,000 people were concurrently playing on Steam. As of February 14, nearly 60 million hours of gameplay have been watched cumulatively on Twitch. What to expectthe eternal power and light of the Lost Ark. Players can choose from 15 customizable hero classes with a multitude of adaptable and combinable skill sets.

Update 2/16/2022: Amazon Games and Smilegate have announced some updated numbers for Lost Ark's big opening weekend, including a peak of over 1.2 million concurrent viewers on Twitch and over 59.9 million hours watched cumulatively during launch week. Additionally, in the first three days of its launch, Lost Ark gained over 4.7 million new registered players in the West. Original story: Following its official free-to-play launch on February 11, Lost Ark has already become Steam's second most-played game of all time by concurrent players with an all-time peak of 1,325,305 people. As revealed by SteamDB, Lost Ark passed Counter-Strike: Global Offensive's all-time peak of 1,308,963 players to secure the #2 spot. Considering these all-time peaks tend to happen around a game's release, it may be quite an uphill battle to take down PUBG's record of 3,257,248 players at one time. That aside, it's an impressive feat for the MMO that is developed by Smilegate and Tripod Studio and published by Amazon Game Studios. This accomplishment shouldn't come as too much of a surprise as Lost Ark had already become one of Steam's biggest-ever games before it even went free-to-play.  As with many MMOs or online games, there were some server issues as millions of players attempted to play Lost Ark at once.

You can download and run Lost Ark for free, and all of its in-app purchases are optional, such as “Starter Packs” that offer large amounts of in-game currency and a month-long “buff,” Crystalline Aura, that makes your character’s life a little easier. The most expensive pack also includes several cosmetic bonuses, like special character costumes and a special warhorse mount. Even so, it’s worth noting that Lost Ark, like New World, is coming out at an opportune time. As PC Gamer suggests, Lost Ark is as much a hack-and-slash dungeon crawler (think Diablo) as it is a traditional MMORPG, and it does an adequate job balancing the strengths of both genres at once. Fans of addictive action-RPGs for the PC haven’t gotten much else to get excited about lately besides expansions for Path of Exile, so many might have migrated over to Lost Ark just to scratch the same “murder entire hordes of monsters at once” itch. As such, Lost Ark is running against limited competition. The previous record-holder for concurrent users of an MMORPG on Steam is, in fact, Amazon’s own New World, which topped out at just over 913,000 simultaneous players just after its launch in September. Lost Ark also doesn’t cost anything to play, which is always going to be a boost for any game’s user numbers.

query: What medals did Jessie Diggins win in the Beijing 2022 Olympic Games?
positive: VTDigger reports that Jessie Diggins, trained in Stratton, overcame food poisoning on Sunday to secure her second medal at the 2022 Beijing Winter Games. The 30-year-old cross-country skier previously achieved gold in the individual sprint on February 8 and subsequently earned a bronze in the 30-kilometer freestyle race. In an interview with NBC, Diggins described the experience as profoundly emotional, stating, “That was one of the hardest things I’ve ever done in my whole life, especially because I had food poisoning 30 hours ago, which is why I thought I was going to die at the finish line. My legs were cramping the whole last 17 kilometers. I don’t know how I made it.” With this achievement, Diggins, who secured silver in the 2018 women’s team sprint, now possesses an Olympic medal in each color. She elaborated to reporters, “It’s been an emotional roller-coaster, but I am so happy we made it to the end. To have a medal in the sprint and the 30K are the ultimate bookends for me. I have been trying to be a good all-round athlete my whole life, so this has been really cool.” Diggins completed the final cross-country race of the Beijing Olympics in a time of 1 hour 26 minutes and 37 seconds, finishing less than two minutes behind Norway's Therese Johaug and 50 seconds ahead of Finland's Kerttu Niskanen, who placed third.

Jessie Diggins secured the bronze medal in the 30km mass start event, thereby becoming the first American cross-country skier to attain two medals at a single Olympic Winter Games. Her performance at the Beijing 2022 Olympics on Sunday further solidified her status as the United States' preeminent cross-country skier. At the age of 30, Diggins finished third, trailing Norway's Therese Johaug, who achieved her third individual title at these Games, contributing to Norway's overall tally of 25 medals. Prior to this event, Diggins had already earned a silver medal in the individual sprint, marking her as the first American woman to win a medal in cross-country skiing and establishing her as the first American cross-country skier to win two medals at a single Olympic Games. This achievement adds to her historical significance, as she previously secured Team USA's inaugural gold medal in the sport alongside Kikkan Randall in the team sprint at the PyeongChang 2018 Olympics. Notably, Diggins experienced food poisoning prior to her final event, which she described to Olympics.com, stating that she had minimal confidence entering the race due to her condition, as she spent the preceding day resting and consuming fluids. She remarked, "If I'm being honest, 24 hours ago I felt really bad and I just laid in bed yesterday mostly and ate food and drank sport drinks. But it worked and I think maybe because it's been so cold and so hard, maybe extra rest is what I needed." In light of her suboptimal preparation, her immediate collapse upon crossing the finish line was perhaps predictable.

During the 2017 Nordic World Ski Championships held in Lahti, Finland, Diggins earned two medals: she won the gold medal in the freestyle sprint after triumphing in both her quarterfinal and semifinal heats, finishing ahead of her teammate Randall, who placed third. Furthermore, in the classic team sprint event, Diggins, alongside Sadie Bjornsen, finished in fourth place, successfully overtaking the Swedish team in the final moments of the race to claim the silver medal by a margin of 0.19 seconds. This accomplishment marked Diggins as the first American to secure four World Championship medals in the discipline of cross-country skiing. In the 2017–2018 Tour de Ski, Diggins concluded the competition in third place overall, making her the first American to achieve a podium finish in the overall classification and surpassing her previous best of fifth place in the prior edition. Her teammate Sadie Bjornsen finished in ninth place overall, marking the inaugural instance in which two American competitors placed within the overall top ten. Additionally, Diggins attained second place in the World Cup standings for the 2017–2018 season. At the 2018 Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang, South Korea, Diggins and Randall distinguished themselves as the first American cross-country skiers to win a silver medal, succeeding in the women's team sprint at the Alpensia Cross-Country Centre.

Jessie Diggins achieved a significant milestone by securing third place in the women's 1.5-kilometer freestyle sprint, finishing behind Swedish athletes Jonna Sundling and Maja Dahlqvist. This notable performance marks Diggins as the first American woman to obtain an Olympic medal in an individual cross-country skiing event. In a press conference, Diggins expressed her sentiments, stating, “It’s overwhelming, but in a good way. It’s just really emotional for me because this really belongs to the whole team. It has taken so many years to get here, to have us come and have an individual medal. And that’s something that doesn’t happen alone. It doesn’t happen without a lot of help. So I’m just so thankful for our team – it’s been a lot of people working really hard for a long time.” This bronze medal complements her previous achievements, including a silver medal won in collaboration with Kikkan Randall in the freestyle team sprint at the 2018 PyeongChang Olympics. Diggins is now recognized as the second American in history to attain an individual Olympic medal in cross-country skiing, following Bill Koch, who won gold in the 30-kilometer event during the 1976 Games in Innsbruck, Austria. Furthermore, she is the only American cross-country skier to have earned multiple Olympic medals.

Jessie Diggins exhibited remarkable determination to secure the gold medal in the women's 30k freestyle event on Sunday, marking her second medal at the 2022 Olympic Winter Games. Rosie Brennan narrowly missed the podium for the second consecutive occasion at these Games, finishing in sixth place, while Sophia Laukli achieved a commendable 15th position in her Olympic debut, and Novie McCabe rounded out the results in 18th place—representing the highest-ever cross-country performance by American women at the Olympic Winter Games. Norway's Therese Johaug secured her third silver medal of the Games with a finishing time of 1:24:45, while Diggins trailed by 1:23. Finland's Kerttu Niskanen led a strong pursuit in the final kilometers to capture the gold, finishing 2:43 behind. Despite experiencing food poisoning the previous day, Diggins demonstrated resilience, matching the early pace and ultimately securing her position in the leading group during the first of four 7.5k laps. She reflected on her preparation, stating, “I went from having not the race prep I had envisioned - laying in bed, force-feeding myself oatmeal and soup from a can - to here we go. But I just wanted to race because I love to race.”

Jessica Diggins, born on August 26, 1991, is a prominent American cross-country skier. Alongside her teammate Kikkan Randall, she achieved a historic milestone by securing the United States' inaugural gold medal in cross-country skiing during the team sprint event at the 2018 Winter Olympics. In the subsequent 2022 Winter Olympics, Diggins further distinguished herself by winning a gold medal in the 30-kilometer freestyle event and a silver medal in the individual sprint, thereby establishing herself as the most decorated American cross-country skier to date. Between 2013 and 2017, she earned four medals at the FIS Nordic World Ski Championships and subsequently became the first American to win a gold medal in an individual event at this championship in 2023. In 2021, Diggins achieved the significant accomplishment of finishing first in the women's overall standings of the 2020–21 FIS Cross-Country World Cup, marking her as the first American to attain this position. Born in Saint Paul, Minnesota, and raised in Afton, Minnesota, Diggins has one sister named Mackenzie. She commenced her skiing journey at the age of four and demonstrated notable skill at the age of 11 when she began competing against older peers. Diggins completed her secondary education at Stillwater Area High School in 2010 and was an active member of the school's cross-country ski team.

On February 3, 2023, following the conclusion of the Games, Team USA achieved a total of 25 medals, comprising eight gold, twelve silver, and five bronze. This tally represented the third-highest number of medals earned by any participating nation.

positive: The Endgame is a television series categorized as an American romance drama thriller, which premiered on NBC on February 21, 2022. The series was created by Nicholas Wootton and Jake Coburn. Following its initial season, the series was canceled in May 2022. The narrative centers around the character of Elena Federova, a criminal mastermind, who confronts Val Turner, a principled FBI agent. Prior to the series' launch, on April 21, 2021, NBC issued a pilot order for The Untitled Nick Wootton/Jake Coburn Project. Wootton and Coburn were anticipated to serve as executive producers, alongside Julie Plec and Emily Cummins, with Wootton also responsible for writing the pilot episode. Subsequently, on September 21, 2021, NBC greenlit the project for a full series. Justin Lin and Andrew Schneider were introduced as additional executive producers, with Lin also directing the pilot episode. The production of the series involved several companies, including Universal Television, Nicholas Wootton Productions, Jake Coburn Productions, Inc., My So-Called Company, and Perfect Storm Entertainment. Following the series order, a cast featuring Morena Baccarin, Ryan Michelle Bathé, Kamal Angelo Bolden, Costa Ronin, Noah Bean, Jordan Johnson-Hinds, and Mark D. Espinoza was assembled in prominent roles. Additionally, on November 18, 2021, Karl Josef Co and Massiel Mordan were incorporated into the cast.

"The Fall" exemplifies a narrative that centers on the intellectual confrontation between law enforcement agents and a serial killer. Additionally, it boasts a formidable cast, with Gillian Anderson delivering one of her most noteworthy performances. Anderson's compelling presence is complemented by Jamie Dornan's portrayal of the serial killer, in which he embodies a familiar charm and charisma. While there are instances in which the series may experience fluctuations in its pacing, it undeniably serves as a provocative investigation into the criminal psyche. The character of Hannibal Lecter stands out as one of the most renowned fictional serial killers, having appeared across various media, including literature, film, and the television series that bears his name. Despite their roles as adversaries, the relationship between the two characters constitutes the emotional nucleus of the series. This series, recognized as one of Netflix's most exemplary productions, centers on a formidable figure alongside the individual tasked with his elimination, notably Javier Pena, portrayed by Pedro Pascal in a highly acclaimed performance. Additionally, the final show on this list offers a distinct perspective on the heist genre by centering on the victims—specifically, the hostages involved in a bank caught in a 52-hour standoff. negative: Those who enjoy the cat and mouse thriller The Endgame will be glad to know that there are a number of other similar television shows. The thriller genre is one that has been experiencing a bit of a golden age, and it seems that almost every network wants to have one of its own. It’s actually easy to see why the genre would continue to maintain its popularity. RELATED: 10 Thriller Movies With The Best Re-Watch Value More than almost any other genre, it aims to keep the audience on the edge of their seat, constantly wondering just what’s going to happen and how the mystery (or mysteries) will ultimately be resolved. The Endgame is the newest thriller to grace NBC, and it joins a large number of similar series, both on that network and elsewhere. There is much about The Blacklist that will appeal to those who also enjoyed The Endgame. Most notably, there is the central conflict between its two main characters, Raymond Reddington and Elizabeth Keene. It’s a densely woven series, with a number of enigmas and mysteries that it constantly keeps in the air. There’s no denying, though, that a major part of its appeal stems from James Spader’s scenery-chewing performance as the enigmatic yet compelling Reddington, and it remains one of his best roles. American popular culture has an enduring fascination with the figure of the drug lord, and there are few drug lords who are as renowned as Pablo Escobar.

The show centered on a professional thief named Mickey O’Neil (played by the dashing Dougray Scott), the man who was orchestrating probably one of the biggest heists that Los Angeles – and maybe even the world – had ever seen. Mickey put together a handpicked team of experts to simultaneously rob three big jewelry stores along Rodeo Drive during the Academy Awards when everyone was too distracted by the glitz and glamor of Hollywood’s elite to notice the elaborate heist unfold.    NBC’s newest foray into the heist TV drama genre is The Endgame which just recently premiered on the network. Starring Morena Baccarin and Ryan Michelle Bathé, The Endgame follows Elena Federova, an international arms dealer and criminal mastermind who was recently captured by the FBI. Elena has orchestrated a number of bank heists all across New York and nobody knows why. But FBI agent Val Turner gets put on the case and might just be able to foil Elena’s ambitious plan and discover what her endgame is.    The only thing better than watching a heist unfold on a scripted TV show is watching it unfold knowing it’s completely based on real-life events. That’s exactly the case with ITV’s four-part miniseries titled Hatton Garden (also known as The Heist at Hatton Garden), which is based on the real-life burglary of a safety deposit vault in the eponymous Hatton Garden, London.

Avengers: Endgame is a 2019 American superhero film based on the Marvel Comics superhero team the Avengers. Produced by Marvel Studios and distributed by Walt Disney Studios Motion Pictures, it is the direct sequel to Avengers: Infinity War (2018) and the 22nd film in the Marvel Cinematic Universe (MCU). Directed by Anthony and Joe Russo and written by Christopher Markus and Stephen McFeely, the film features an ensemble cast including Robert Downey Jr., Chris Evans, Mark Ruffalo, Chris Hemsworth, Scarlett Johansson, Jeremy Renner, Don Cheadle, Paul Rudd, Brie Larson, Karen Gillan, Danai Gurira, Benedict Wong, Jon Favreau, Bradley Cooper, Gwyneth Paltrow, and Josh Brolin. In the film, the surviving members of the Avengers and their allies attempt to reverse Thanos's actions in Infinity War.  The film was announced in October 2014 as Avengers: Infinity War – Part 2, but Marvel later removed this title. The Russo brothers joined as directors in April 2015, with Markus and McFeely signing on to write the script a month later. The film serves as a conclusion to the story of the MCU up to that point, ending the story arcs for several main characters. The plot revisits several moments from earlier films, bringing back actors and settings from throughout the franchise.

October 30, 2020		 • 3  For the last decade, Marvel has led the adaptations of superhero comic books into films. In 2019, the journey culminated in the release of Avengers: Endgame, which premiered to critical and commercial success. It had gone from a story about a playboy billionaire (Tony Stark/Iron Man) to a larger story than spanned universes as well as heroes and villains alike. Although, as the universe expanded, it was hard to ignore that Marvel’s roster of superheroes still lacked diversity. This was made even more apparent when other superhero stories started to make their way into the mainstream.  In the same year that Endgame came out, a quiet and subversive TV show was making waves. The Boys is adapted from a graphic novel of the same name, one that is notorious amongst fans and haters alike for its crude, graphic and violent nature. The series considers what would happen if superheroes existed in the real world. The short answer is that they would become an untouchable commodity that could wreak havoc, which would be cleaned up by their slick and all-powerful parent company Vought. The series touches on power, social media, politics and causes the line between hero and villain to become truly blurred.  It is difficult not to draw comparisons between the storylines in The Boys and the reality of the MCU (Marvel Cinematic Universe).

Not that it's really over, of course: Disney and Marvel are still working out new wrinkles in the time-money continuum. But the Russos do provide the sense of an ending, a chance to appreciate what has been done before the timelines reset and we all get back to work."[196] Justin Chang of the Los Angeles Times wrote that "Avengers: Endgame achieves and earns its climactic surge of feeling, even as it falls just short of real catharsis".[197] Some have noted the film as a notable improvement over its predecessor, Avengers: Infinity War, such as Brian Tallerico of RogerEbert.com, who stated that Endgame is "a more patient, focused film [than Infinity War], even as its plot draws in elements of a dozen other movies."[198] Matt Zoller Seitz, also of RogerEbert.com, gave the film a positive assessment as compared with Infinity War, which he considered "too crowded, too rushed and yet too long". Seitz stated that Endgame is "a heartfelt and satisfying experience", along with being a "surprisingly relaxed, character-driven, self-aware yet sincere comedy [for] two-thirds of [the film].

Much of the script suggests a laid-back Richard Linklater movie with superheroes".[199] Joe Morgenstern of The Wall Street Journal acknowledged the unique achievement that Avengers: Endgame accomplished as the conclusion of the Infinity Saga, calling the final battle "inevitably unwieldy [...] but thrilling all the same, and followed by a delicate coda. So many stories. So many adventures. So much to sort out before the next cycle starts."[200]  Richard Brody, writing for The New Yorker, was more critical of the film, opining that the good acting was not matched by comparable skill from the directors. He said, "The Russos have peculiarly little sense of visual pleasure, little sense of beauty, little sense of metaphor, little aptitude for texture or composition; their spectacular conceit is purely one of scale, which is why their finest moments are quiet and dramatic ones".[201] Anthony Lane of The New Yorker gave the film a compromising review, finding it to be overdeveloped and overwrought, stating, "The one thing you do need to know about Avengers: Endgame is that it runs for a little over three hours, and that you can easily duck out during the middle hour, do some shopping, and slip back into your seat for the climax. You won't have missed a thing."[202]  At the 92nd Academy Awards, Avengers: Endgame received a nomination for Best Visual Effects.
